coredata.dc:title: Carrageenan-based functional films hybridized with carbon dots and anthocyanins from rose petals for smart food packaging applications

coredata.dc:description: Multifunctional smart biopolymeric films were fabricated using rose petal anthocyanin (RPA) and carrageenan (CAR) doped with rose petal-derived carbon dots (RP-CDs). Response surface-optimized RPA showed the highest total anthocyanins and radical scavenging ability. Produced RP-CD exhibited UV absorption and high fluorescence with antibacterial/antioxidant abilities. Enrichment with 2 % RP-CD and 5 % RPA in the CAR matrix results in improved physicochemical, i.e., water contact angle, water vapor permeability, and UV-blocking properties of the fabricated material. Results showed that nanocomposite films scavenged radicals better than the neat CAR films. Zeta potential, FTIR, SEM, and XPS suggested improved compatibility/stability and enhanced elemental configuration of RP-CDs/RPA additives in the CAR polymer matrix. Perishable food packaging (minced pork and shrimp) demonstrated that nanocomposite films work efficiently and non-destructively and are promising tools for monitoring real-time freshness through interpretable visual changes from red to yellow. The CAR/RP-CDs/RPA-based nanocomposite indicator films are expected to be applied as various smart packaging materials. These films possess the ability to promptly detect changes in quality, preserve the quality, and prolong the shelf life of packaged foods.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The CAR/RP-CDs/RPA film exhibits higher tensile strength (59.7 MPa) compared to the CAR film (46.9 MPa), which means a statistically significant increase of approximately 12.8 MPa. Incorporation of CAR/RP-CD/RPA into films is likely to enhance intermolecular interactions within the polymer matrix, as supported by SEM and TEM. RP-CD can act as a compatibilizer, improving the dispersion of components and creating more uniform and stronger films [10]. Additionally, the crosslinking effect of RPA may have further contributed to the increase in tensile strength (Fig. 5). Improved tensile strength is a desirable property for packaging films and improves the film's resistance to tearing, puncture and mechanical stress during handling, transportation, and storage. Elongation at break is a measure of a material's ability to stretch before breaking. The higher the elongation at break, the better the flexibility [43]. As shown in Table 2, the elongation at the break of CAR films (3.8 \u00B1 0.3 %) was lower than that of CAR/RP-CDs/RPA films (5.1 \u00B1 0.5 %), indicating that the flexibility of the film increased by the addition of RP-CDs/RPA.The work was executed under the financial assistance for the International training provided by the Indian Council of Agricultural Research (ICAR), New Delhi, India, through the National Agricultural Higher Education Project (IDP-NAHEP), Guru Angad Dev Veterinary and Animal Sciences University, Ludhiana, Punjab (India). This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) (2022R1A2B5B02001422).

The work was executed under the financial assistance for the International training provided by the Indian Council of Agricultural Research ( ICAR ), New Delhi, India, through the National Agricultural Higher Education Project ( IDP-NAHEP ), Guru Angad Dev Veterinary and Animal Sciences University, Ludhiana, Punjab (India). This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government ( MSIT ) ( 2022R1A2B5B02001422 ).

head.ce:title: Carrageenan-based functional films hybridized with carbon dots and anthocyanins from rose petals for smart food packaging applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Livestock Products Technology, College of Veterinary Science, Guru Angad Dev Veterinary and Animal Sciences University, Ludhiana, Punjab 141004, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guru Angad Dev Veterinary and Animal Sciences University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multifunctional smart biopolymeric films were fabricated using rose petal anthocyanin (RPA) and carrageenan (CAR) doped with rose petal-derived carbon dots (RP-CDs). Response surface-optimized RPA showed the highest total anthocyanins and radical scavenging ability. Produced RP-CD exhibited UV absorption and high fluorescence with antibacterial/antioxidant abilities. Enrichment with 2 % RP-CD and 5 % RPA in the CAR matrix results in improved physicochemical, i.e., water contact angle, water vapor permeability, and UV-blocking properties of the fabricated material. Results showed that nanocomposite films scavenged radicals better than the neat CAR films. Zeta potential, FTIR, SEM, and XPS suggested improved compatibility/stability and enhanced elemental configuration of RP-CDs/RPA additives in the CAR polymer matrix. Perishable food packaging (minced pork and shrimp) demonstrated that nanocomposite films work efficiently and non-destructively and are promising tools for monitoring real-time freshness through interpretable visual changes from red to yellow. The CAR/RP-CDs/RPA-based nanocomposite indicator films are expected to be applied as various smart packaging materials. These films possess the ability to promptly detect changes in quality, preserve the quality, and prolong the shelf life of packaged foods.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As the environmental waste problem caused by petrochemical-based, non-degradable plastic packaging materials becomes more serious, the demand for biodegradable packaging materials that can replace them is increasing significantly [1]. However, inherent disadvantages of biodegradable polymer-based packaging constituents, like low mechanical attributes, lack of antibacterial/antioxidant functions, and UV-blocking, have led to their application to nanocomposite-based packaging technology [2]. Nanocomposite food packaging is a next-generation packaging technology based on nanomaterials and is currently one of the most advanced fields of nanotechnology [3,4]. Biopolymer-based nanocomposites are hybrid constituents consisting of a natural polymer matrix reinforced with nanodots/flakes/tubes/diamonds or nanosized (<100 nm) particles [5], which enhances the functionality of the resulting packaging film [2,6,7].

Carbon (nano) dots (CD), which were accidentally discovered by Xu et al. [8] in 2004 while purifying carbon nanotubes, are attracting great attention as a new type of nanomaterial. CDs are zero-dimensional optically active carbon-based nanomaterials with a size of <10 nm [6]. Later, CDs were manufactured using various raw materials, and recently, a method of synthesizing CDs using biological waste resources has been actively conducted [9,10]. The function of CD as an active nanocomposite in food packaging is extensively used as a UV-blocking, antibacterial agent, and antioxidant agent [5]. The capacity of these packages to enhance the mechanical characteristics of the film and inhibit microbiological contamination/growth guarantees the long-term safety of food. Most importantly, it protects the biopolymer from microbial degradation.

A significant proportion of edible food is wasted in the supply chain each year, and consumer food poisoning due to oxidation and microbial spoilage places an additional financial burden on healthcare systems [11]. Therefore, consumers and food industry stakeholders have expressed great interest in using simple, non-destructive, and inexpensive methods to control/maintain or monitor the freshness of perishable foods. The idea of smart packaging has been introduced to address these requirements satisfactorily. Smart packaging, which revolutionizes conventional food packaging, incorporates advanced devices and indicators like temperature and time indicators (TTI) [12] and pH indicators [13,14], incorporated into the packaging film or affixed on the interior of the container. Carotenoids, anthocyanins and betacyanins are pH-based natural markers that have received considerable attention from the food industry and the scientific community due to their safety and cost-effectiveness.

The genus Rosa, family Rosaceae the most popular ornamental and edible flowering plant and is cultivated in many parts of the world [15]. Rose petals are a well-known source of phenolic acids, flavonoids, anthocyanins, and carotenoids [16]. Major anthocyanins, mainly found in edible roses, include cyanidin and pelargonidin, which exhibit a characteristic halo discoloration at various pHs [12,13]. This natural pH/halochromic indicator option has great potential due to its wide range of color responses, making it particularly suitable for intelligent applications in food packaging [17].

This study aimed to fabricate and characterize smart carrageenan (CAR)-based nanocomposite indicator films engineered with response surface-optimized rose petal anthocyanin (RPA). To this end, biowaste-derived RP-CD (prepared using rose petals left after anthocyanin extraction) was fabricated to functionalize CAR polymer and develop an active and smart indicator film, and the efficacy of this multifunctional color-changeable nanocomposite indicator film was demonstrated through real-time quality monitoring at 25 °C using minced pork and shrimp.

Fresh red roses (Rosa hybrida L) were procured from a local flower market (Seoul, Republic of Korea). κ-carrageenan (CAR), Hankook, Jeonnam, Seoul, Rep. Korea. Pure culture of Salmonella enterica, Listeria monocytogenes (L. monocytogenes), Escherichia coli (E. coli) O157: H7, and Staphylococcus aureus availed from the Korean Collection for Type Culture, Seoul, Korea. Analytical chemicals were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Luis, MO, USA) and Daejung Chemicals Co., Ltd. (Siheung, Republic of Korea).

Optimization of extraction of RP anthocyanins was performed following the method of [18] with minor modifications. RP-CD was synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal method using the filter residue of rose petals obtained after anthocyanin extraction, as shown in Scheme 1 .

Film solutions were prepared by dissolving 4 g of CAR in 180 mL of distilled water and 30 wt% (based on polymer) glycerol, with constant stirring at 75 °C for 45 min, and cooled to 40 °C to add 5.0 wt% RPA and 2.0 wt% RP-CD, which were determined as optimal conditions through preliminary experiments. The solution was then cast onto Teflon (25 cm × 32 cm) coated plates, followed by drying at 24 ± 3 °C for 48 h. The dried films were peeled off and conditioned at 25 °C/50 % relative humidity until further use. Control films were prepared without adding RP-CDs/RPA. The fabricated films were designated as CAR and CAR/RP-CDs/RPA films, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence and UV–vis fluorescence and TEM properties of RP-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescence (PL) measurement of RP-CDs was performed using a spectrophotometer (Fluoromax, Horiba Inc., MA, USA). The UV–Vis spectrum was determined using a spectrophotometer (OptizenM, Seoul, Republic of Korea). ImageJ (Ver. 1.53u) was used to determine size distribution and average particle size. The morphological characteristics of RP-CDs were carried out using a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (FE-TEM, JEM-2100F, Jeol Ltd., Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Zeta potential, FTIR, XPS, and SEM analysis of RP-CDs and indicator films
ce:section.ce:para: The zeta potential analyzer was employed to measure the surface charge on the particles and their stability in aqueous suspension, and the dynamic light scattering analyzer determined the particle size distribution (Zetasizer, Nano-ZS-ZEN, Malvern Instr. Pvt. Ltd., UK). The CD prepared for this purpose was mixed with water and diluted 1000 times. Dilutions were sieved through a 0.2 μm filter before analysis.

An ATR-FTIR spectrophotometer (TENSOR™, Bruker-Optics Inc., USA) was used to characterize the chemical structures of the RP-CDs and film samples in the range 4000–500 cm−1 with an average resolution of 32 scans at 4 cm−1.
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a K-Alpha X-ray spectrometer (Thermo-Scientific Co. Ltd., USA) to determine the composition of elements of the RP-CDs.

The Morphology of the film surface of film samples and cross-sectional were analyzed using a Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM, S-4700, Hitachi Co., Ltd., Matsuda, Japan), and the microstructure with an atomic force microscope (AFM, NX10, Park Systems, Gyeonggi-do, Korea).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant and antibacterial efficacy of RP-CDs and indicator films
ce:section.ce:para: The radical scavenging activity of the fabricated films and RP-CDs was estimated using the 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) and 2,2-azino-bis-3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulphonic acid (ABTS) methods [19]. The total anthocyanins (TAC) were estimated following Alizadeh Sani et al. [20] and were expressed as mg Cy3G/100 g FW. The antibacterial efficiency of RP-CDs was investigated using the diffusion method (agar well) against selected food-borne pathogens.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and mechanical properties of the film
ce:section.ce:para: The optical attributes in terms of surface color (L, a, and b) of the film were measured using Konica Minolta, CR-400, Tokyo, Japan.

The thickness of the films was noted with a micrometer (Quantu-65-Mike, Mitutoyo Pvt., Japan) at different locations of the films (n = 6). Tensile strength (TS, MPa) and elongation break (EB, %) using Instron Universal Testing Machine (Model 5564, Instron Engineering Corporation, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water vapor permeability (WVP), Contact angle (WCA), and thermal stability of the film
ce:section.ce:para: The WVP of the films was determined using a gravimetric method, and the WCA of the films was determined using a CA analyzer (Phenix 150, Surface Electro Optics Co., Kunpo, Korea) following the previously reported methods [10]. Thermal stability was evaluated using a Hi-Res TGA- thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA-2950, T.A. Inc., DE, USA).

The sensitivity of the indicator films was tested by simulation (in-vivo) studies using acid and base gases. For this, the indicator was affixed over an acetic acid (99 %) and ammonia (0.8 M) solution for 15 min, and then the halochromic response of the developed film was recorded using a chroma meter [21].

Real-time quality monitoring of a perishable food storage model consisting of minced pork (large white Yorkshire) and shrimp (Fenneropenaeus chinensis) during storage was studied. A developed indicator film (3.2 cm × 3.2 cm) was fixed on the inner surface of the packaging lid, and about 112 g of fresh samples of minced pork and shrimp were packaged in a glass box (17.5 cm × 12.5 cm × 3.5 cm), and then kept at 25 °C for 48 h and checked color changes at every 12 h intervals. The pH related to stored samples was tested by homogenizing a 12 g sample with 90 mL of water (IKA T50 digital Ultra Turrax, Germany) and measuring the pH of the resulting slurry using a digital pH meter (A-211 pH meter, Thermo Scientific, Indonesia). The photographs of the indicator film were acquired utilizing a digital camera (Samsung Electronics, Seoul, Korea). The chroma meter (CR-400, Konica, Minolta, Tokyo, Japan) was used to calculate the indicator film's total color difference (ΔE). Extract release value (ERV) was determined as described by Jay and Kontou [10,22]. A consumer panel of 11 semi-trained members performed a sensory evaluation of the overall acceptability (OA) of stored food models at regular intervals of 12 h.

All experiments were performed in triplicates, and the experimental data were presented as mean ± standard deviation. Data were statistically analyzed using SPSS-24 and Design-Expert software (IBM-SPSS, NY, USA and Stat-Ease, Inc., USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of extraction protocols for RPA
ce:section.ce:para: Preliminary trials were conducted to select the best extractable solvents to obtain higher yields of active extracts from rose petals. Based on preliminary trials 80 % ethanol was selected as solvent for experiments, encoding and noncoding factors, and analyzed the data using Design Expert software. A Box-Behnken design (BBD) proposed 17 experimental runs with different treatment protocols, such as solvent concentration, vacuum evaporator extraction time, and temperature for the response, namely TAC (mg Cy3G/100 g FW), ABTS (%), and DPPH (%). Based on the obtained results, analysis of variance, i.e., ANOVA, was plotted with 3D surface plots. The data obtained were fitted to a second-order polynomial. ANOVA suggested that the model had a high F-value and a low p-value (< 0.0001) for the study response (Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: TAC, DPPH, and ABTS scavenging activity
ce:section.ce:para:
Resultant ANOVA showed the relationship between TAC, DPPH and ABTS scavenging activities with the studied factor was quadratic with good regression coefficients. The model showed relationships according to the equations below;
(1)

The ANOVA results showed that A (ethanolic concentration), B (time), C (temperature), A2, B2, C2 are (p < 0.05) model terms for TAC contents, and A, C, B, A2, B2, C2 are p < 0.05 model terms for DPPH, and A, B, C, AC, A2, B2, C2 term effects are p < 0.05 for ABTS. The term's effects suggested varying ethanolic concentrations (50–80 %), extracting time (5–15 min), and temperature (60–90 °C) affected yield and radical activity of bioactive compounds from red rose petals.

The relationship between temperature and time can be hypothesized utilizing three processes (Fig. 2) [28]. Initially, when the interaction between lignin and phenolic acids is disrupted due to mild heating, phenolic compounds (namely cyanidin) that are not soluble may be liberated by deglycosylation [29]. Second, because of higher mass solubility and diffusion coefficient at elevated heat, lignin and phenolic (insoluble) may decompose [24], resulting in more phenol intermediates like hydroxybenzoic acid and phloroglucinaldehyde [30]. Lastly, prolonged exposure to high temperatures may result in the thermal destruction of polyphenols [25], as shown in Fig. 2. However, upper-temperature limitations can be suggested to prevent the breakdown of thermo-sensitive substances [24]. The recommended anthocyanin extraction temperature has been reported to be <80 °C, which agrees with previous findings [24,31].

The maximum DPPH, TAC, and ABTS activities were 159.2 mg of Cy3G/100 g of TAC, 81.5 % of ABTS, and 70.1 % of DPPH, respectively, at an extraction solution of 68 %, extraction time of 11.15 min, and extraction temperature of 73.7 °C.

Anthocyanin-rich extracts from rose petals were prepared using an optimized extraction protocol and further used to develop a fluorescent color indicator. In addition, the pH-dependent reaction of the obtained red pose extract is to be verified. It has been applied to various pH buffer solutions from 2 to 12. The results of the experiments (Fig. 2b) revealed surprising and substantial reactions and differences at the different pH tests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–Vis fluorescence, TEM and Photoluminescence attributes of RP-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy (excitation-dependent fluorescence emission) was used to evaluate the properties related to optical attributes. PL spectroscopy is mainly used to study the luminescent properties and characterize the CDs [32]. As the excitation wavelength increased, the fluorescence emission shifted to higher wavelengths (Fig. 3a), indicating the emission of RP-CDs relating to excitation [10,32]. The fluorescence spectrum of RP-CD showed potent fluorescence at 436 nm (emission) with an excitation at 360 nm (Fig. 3b).

According to the UV–Vis spectrophotometry data, the synthesized RP-CDs are good photon harvesters owing to the spectral absorption of conjugated protons in the sp2 atomic configuration, particularly in the short wavelength zone [6], which might be correlated to the electron transition from π to π* of the CC bonds and n to π* of the CO bonds at the edges of RP-CD. The solution exhibited bright blue scattered emission (UV, 365 nm) but appeared completely translucent under visible light (Fig. 3b). However, the light-emitting mechanism of carbon-based quantum dots has not yet been elucidated.

The excitation-dependent PL is supposed to be due to the synergetic consequence of edge/trap states and quantum confinement (nanoscale effect). The excitation-dependent PL and UV absorption properties of RP-CDs have established them as active candidates for fabricating UV-blocking films [5].

Under UV illumination, CDs (5–10 nm in size) are well known to be potent photon emitters [5,33]. The upsurge in PL phot-intensity may be due to the functional groups responsible for the fluorescence behavior of the nanocomposite (confirmed by FTIR spectroscopy). These findings are consistent with [33,34].

The morphology (TEM) and particle size of RP-CDs are spherical and evenly distributed (Fig. 3c). This pattern might be explained by polar-polar interactions between the surface's various hydrophilic functional groups [28]. In addition, the histogram graph (Fig. 3d) related to particle size distribution showed that the diameter of the obtained carbon dots varied from 2.23 to 4.1 nm (average diameter: 3.36 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Zeta potential, FTIR, and XPS analysis of RP-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In the characterization of the interface electrochemical equilibrium or stability of the solution, Zeta potential is a proven powerful tool [35]. A zeta potential test was performed to evaluate the stability and quantity of the surface charge of RP-CDs in an aqueous solution. RP-CD exhibited a negative surface charge with a value of −23.1 mV. The high negative charge of RP-CD can create a repulsive force between RP-CD, resulting in greater stabilization [36,37]. Conversely, the negative zeta potential of synthesized CDs can be associated with negatively charged efficient groups like amino, carboxylic acid, and hydroxyl groups at the edges of CDs, as confirmed by FTIR and XPS [33]. Therefore, the high negative zeta potential of RP-CDs is a desirable quality, showing high stability and active surface sites that can contribute to the functional properties of CDs. Similar negative zeta potential assays of CDs have been testified to be −5.44 mV in CDs synthesized from natural peony powder [38] and − 28.3 mV prepared using peony pollen.

FTIR spectrometry is mostly employed for the characterization of the functional groups of nanomaterials like carbon dots [35]. The stretching and bending oscillations of Fourier transformation infrared peaks recognize the functional groups of carbon dots.

The signal peaks at 3308, 2971, 1633, 1392, 1247, and 1060 cm−1 in the spectra (Fig. 4a) leads to the OH, CH, CC, COOH, and C=O/C-OH groups, respectively [35]. As a result of the oxygen-bearing functional groups, RP-CDs are easily dispersible in water and form stable suspensions (as confirmed by the zeta potential). These results demonstrated yielded CDs have excellent water receptibility and biocompatibility for further applications.

Furthermore, XPS spectra demonstrated the RP-CDs' surface state and elementary composition (Fig. 4b). Peaks at 531, 399, and 284 eV designated the existence of N 1 s, O 1 s, and C 1 s, respectively [39]. The deconvoluted spectra for C1s display three peaks (Fig. 4c), out of which the major binding peak at 284 eV might be due to the graphitic carbon (sp2) structure of the synthesized RP-CDs, demonstrating the presence of CC and CC. Fig. 4d shows high-resolution spectra of N1s displaying four peaks around 399, which links to CN, C=N/C-N-C. The deconvoluted spectra of O1s at 531 eV revealed the existence of N-C=O/C=O and -C-OH (Fig. 4e). These results showed that the RP-CDs were mainly composed of the elements C1s, O1s, and N1s at relative percentage compositions of 68.55 %, 23.60 %, and 7.41 %, respectively, verifying several functional surface groups on the RP-CDs [10]. The obtained XPS findings are consistent with the FTIR findings.

CDs have been among the most extensively researched antimicrobial alternatives in recent years. The antibacterial effectiveness of the synthesized RP-CD was tested using Gram-negative (E. coli and Salmonella enterica) and Gram-positive (L. monocytogenes, Staphylococcus aureus) pathogenic bacteria using the agar well diffusion method (Fig. S1). RP-CD showed the largest inhibition area (>28 mm) for Staphylococcus aureus, followed by Salmonella enterica and L. monocytogenes, and E. coli showed the least inhibition area (∼3 mm) at 100 μL concentration.

The mechanism behind the bactericidal action of CD is attributed to excitation allied with π → π* in the central core of the CDs [38]., inducing the formation of highly reactive singlet oxygen species, generating radicals like •OH and O2•-, which could lead to damage to microbes, including permeability of cell membrane [9], damage to DNA, and cytoplasmic leakage, etc.

Fig. S2 shows that the free radical scavenging activity increased gradually with the increasing concentration of the carbon dots from 12.5 μg/mL to 100 μg/mL [10,19]. The dose-dependent increased radical scavenging activity is attributed to the enhanced electron transfer potential of RP-CDs to the free radical in ABTS/DPPH [9]. These findings are consistent with previous results reporting higher antioxidant scavenging efficiency in CDs [5,10]. The RP-CDs are expected to be used as a natural, powerful antioxidant in food packaging.

The FTIR (Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy) results depicted in Fig. 4a of the pure CAR and CAR/RP-CDs/RPA films revealed the presence of wide peaks at 3310–17 cm-1, representing H- and O ̶H stretching bonding [40]. The peaks at 2924–2927 cm−1 were ascribed to C ̶ H stretching [35,40]. The peaks at 1026–1027 cm−1 represent C-OH vibrations caused by CAR polymer. As detailed above, results suggested good surface interaction of RPA and RP-CDs with the CAR polymer via different bonds (Fig. 4a).

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) results of neat CAR and CAR/RP-CD/RPA films are shown in Fig. 4a. In the FTIR spectrum of the neat CAR film, a broad peak at 3310–3317 cm−1 indicated OH stretching bonds [35]. The peak at 2924–2927 cm−1 was attributed to CH stretching [32]. The peaks at 1625, 1213, and 1026 cm−1 were due to the CC, O=S=O, and C-OH vibrations caused by CAR polymer. Characteristic peaks at 918 cm−1 and 840 cm−1 were due to the 3,6-anhydro-D-galactose and the O-SO3 group of galactose-4-sulfate, respectively [41]. After the incorporation of RP-CD and RPA into the CAR matrix, no significant changes were observed in the chemical structure of the polymer, indicating that the incorporation of fillers did not change the structure of the polymer. However, an additional small peak at around 1740 cm−1 was observed in the IR spectrum of CAR/RP-CDs/RPA, which is related to the CO group vibration of the carbon dots, confirming that the carbon dots were well dispersed into the polymer matrix. Additionally, the slight shift in the peak positions of the CAR/RP-CD/RPA spectrum compared to pure CAR may correspond to the intermolecular interactions between the CAR polymer matrix and the added CDs [42].

The SEM studies (Fig. 5a,b) revealed a uniform and opaquer CAR/RP-CDs/RPA than the CAR film, proving that the reinforced RP-CDs enhanced RPA's compatibility with the CAR polymer matrix, perhaps due to the generation of hydrogen bonds [33]. Due to the denser cross-sectional surface (Fig. 5c,d), rose petal anthocyanins were reinforced into CAR composites, meaning that anthocyanins have excellent compatibility and dispersibility in the interstitial spaces of the polymer composites [10]. These changes suggest that the microstructure of the films was significantly influenced by the content added to the polymer, consistent with previously reported results.

Improved uniformity/adhesion can be attributed to the following factors: That is, RPA was prepared from an aqueous solution (20 %) (> polar compound) during extraction experiments [10], and the added RP-CD and CAR matrices can form intermolecular H-bonds to create more compact films [41].

The TGA thermograms of the CAR and indicator films are shown in Fig. 5e,f. The neat CAR and display film showed two stages of weight loss. In the case of pure CAR film, the first weight loss occurred at 78.2 °C, and the indicator film showed the first weight loss at 99.7 °C, which was mainly caused by moisture evaporation [10]. A second heat loss (Fig. 5e,f) was recorded at 235–248 °C (>31 %) due to polymer and glycerol degradation, and the synergistic effect of RPA/RP-CD improved the thermal stability of the film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical, mechanical, physicochemical, and antioxidant properties of the film
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Table 2 , the thickness of the general CAR film is 54 μm, while the thickness of the CAR/RP-CDs/RPA film is 78 μm. The inclusion of solid, dense materials increased the thickness values of the composite films [10,34].

The CAR/RP-CDs/RPA film exhibits higher tensile strength (59.7 MPa) compared to the CAR film (46.9 MPa), which means a statistically significant increase of approximately 12.8 MPa. Incorporation of CAR/RP-CD/RPA into films is likely to enhance intermolecular interactions within the polymer matrix, as supported by SEM and TEM. RP-CD can act as a compatibilizer, improving the dispersion of components and creating more uniform and stronger films [10]. Additionally, the crosslinking effect of RPA may have further contributed to the increase in tensile strength (Fig. 5). Improved tensile strength is a desirable property for packaging films and improves the film's resistance to tearing, puncture and mechanical stress during handling, transportation, and storage. Elongation at break is a measure of a material's ability to stretch before breaking. The higher the elongation at break, the better the flexibility [43]. As shown in Table 2, the elongation at the break of CAR films (3.8 ± 0.3 %) was lower than that of CAR/RP-CDs/RPA films (5.1 ± 0.5 %), indicating that the flexibility of the film increased by the addition of RP-CDs/RPA.

The hydrophobicity or hydrophilicity of a surface can be determined by its water contact angle (WCA) value. If the WCA is <65°, films made from biopolymers are said to be hydrophilic [10,34]. The results showed that the CAR (38°) and CAR/RP-CDs/RPA (49°) films had a WCA of <65° and thus were highly hydrophilic. The notable increase in surface hydrophobicity was due to the improved synergistic crosslinking stiffness of the films, which was enhanced when RPA and RP-CD were added to the CAR matrix, resulting in much higher WCA [43].

A lower water vapor permeation (WVP) is desirable for packing films due to their improved performance in preventing water vapor penetration [10]. As shown in Table 2, compared to the neat CAR films, the WVP of produced indicator films reduced (p < 0.05) when RPA and RP-CDs were dual-doped into CAR. It is possible that these observations, which are supported by SEM data, are caused by the larger aromatic compounds found in the RPA skeletal frameworks. These compounds densified the micro-structural architecture of films and reduced their attraction to water molecules. Additionally, the common molecular structure of RP-CD can promote the cross-network effect in CAR films, limiting the mobility of water vapor molecules and reducing the WVP [41]. The hydrodynamic characteristics of the CAR-nanocomposite films were enhanced by including RPA and RP-CDs.

The results of instrumental color analysis are shown in Table 2. As projected from the UV–vis spectrum (Fig. S3a,b), the control film showed lower L, a, and b color values, representing that the control or neat film was colorless or white opaque.

The UV-blocking properties and transparency of the film were evaluated by light transmittance at 280 nm and 600 nm, respectively. The neat CAR film had very high transparency, showing high light transmittance of 69.5 % for T280 and 89.9 % for T600. However, the T280 of the film decreased significantly with the addition of RP-CD and RPA (p < 0.05), while the T600 decreased slightly. These results indicate that the addition of RP-CD and RPA significantly increased the UV-blocking properties of the films with a slight decrease in transparency. Over the past few years, much attention has been focused on UV-blocking coatings and materials. It is widely known that stored perishable foods discolor, lose nutrients, and oxidatively spoil when exposed to ultraviolet light [44]. Fig. S3(a) shows that UV light transmission was reduced by adding RPA and RP-CDs in the CAR polymer matrix. Firstly, added RPA (5 %) had aromatic rings, which acted as potent UV-blockers [41]. Secondly, RP-CDs possess UV absorbance capacity (Fig. 3). The results indicate that the prepared indicator film can be used as a viable UV-blocking packaging film in addition to its active protection function in food-related packaging systems.

As shown in Fig. S3 (c), the CAR/RP-CDs/RPA films exhibited improved ABTS as well as DPPH abilities compared to the CAR film, likely due to the adding the CAR along with RPA and RP-CDs, showing proven antibacterial/antioxidant capacity (Fig. S1 and S2).

Several volatile chemicals, including ammonia, amines, sulfides, and others, are released during the breakdown of muscle fats/proteins. The response of the CAR/RP-CDs/RPA films to NH3 and acidic gaseous environments was tested in a simulation decay of food investigation utilizing acetic acid (AA) and ammonia (NH3). As depicted in Fig. 6 , exposure of 15 min to NH3 and AA, films showed noticeable color variations. It is worth noting that AA-exposed films showed more redness (10.21) as compared to NH3-exposed films, concluding acidic environment leads to a substantial color shift from a light red hue to dark reddish. This visible change is readily noticeable by the naked eye (Fig. 6a).

The ammonia (NH3)-exposed fabricated films displayed (p < 0.05) higher yellowness (b = 7.68), indicating a yellowish color shift than the neat CAR as well as AA-exposed films (Fig. 6a,b). The volatile gas ammonia interacts with water on exposed surfaces to generate NH3:H2O, releasing ammonium cations and hydroxide [10] and causing an increase in pH and color change. Overall, the fabricated CAR/RP-CD/RPA films were demonstrated to have robust applications from both alkaline and acidic sources by signaling and monitoring the presence of volatile or gaseous ammonia and acidic environments inside food packaging.

Real-time monitoring of quality changes was evaluated using an in vivo model system using minced pork and shrimp. A non-invasive approach was attempted to utilize the invented CAR/RP-CD/RPA film indicator. Fig. 7 shows that quality characteristics such as pH, extractable volume (ERV), total color difference (ΔE), and consumer acceptance were used to evaluate the freshness of an in vivo food model over a 48-h storage period at 25 °C. Sensory evaluation was performed based on the overall acceptability (OA) of consumers, and the color properties of the films were used to understand the complexity of food storage quality indices in vivo.

The pH of both food samples increased significantly with increasing storage time, with shrimp samples increasing the most from 6.41 ± 0.07 to 7.69 ± 0.08 after 48 h and minced pork samples increasing from 5.59 ± 0.04 to 7.01 ± 0.06 (Fig. 7 cd). Minced pork is considered fresh with a pH of 5.18–6.12 and not fresh (6.13–6.16) [24]. Previous investigations have shown that oxidation/microbial degradation begins at pH >7.0 (shrimp) and > 6.8 (pork) [20,44,45]. The development of spoilage chemicals such as trimethylamine, dimethylammonium, NH3 and sugar complexes degrades muscle proteins and lipids, increasing pH during storage [46].

Extractable discharge volume (ERV) can provide a rapid and accurate prediction of meat quality degradation due to microorganisms. Muscle tissue bacteria were negatively associated with extract release volume [10]. Fresh meat/flesh with an ERV >25 mL is permitted [22]. Both pork and shrimp showed acceptable ERV values during the first 24 h of preservation, indicating good quality (Fig. 8 ). As bacterial growth increases during storage, ERV decreases, falling rapidly to below 25 within 48 h of storage.

Consumers judge meat and meat products based on texture, flavor, and appearance [19,46,47], and appearance mostly influences purchases. Semi-trained consumer panels evaluated preserved food models at 12-h intervals (Fig. 7c,d). The overall acceptability (OA) scores of fresh minced pork and shrimp (0 h) were 4.94 and 4.98, respectively. For pork and shrimp, it fell to 2.51 and 2.67 after 24 h. The OA of all food models was >2.5 during 24-h storage [46]. Finally, OA drop (<2.5) in samples after storage for 48 h causes sensory panel rejection, which is in good agreement with previously published results [10].

As discussed, Real-time perishable food freshness is paramount in today's fast-changing lifestyle. The color indices of the developed film containing 5 % RPA and 2 % RP- transformed from red colors at 0 h to dark yellowish at 48 h with the increase in pH (Fig. 7a,b). Interestingly, the b-value or yellowness value of the indicator film increased significantly during storage, and this color change was visible to the naked eye. (Fig. 7a,b). The observed color variations indicate that the newly produced indicator films exhibited intelligent behavior by accurately detecting and responding to quality shifts in stored food models in real time.

The chemical configuration of anthocyanins may vary in accordance with factors like temperature, enzymatic reactions, and pH [24]. The alkaline region exhibits a more pronounced pH-dependent color sensitivity of anthocyanins owing to their increased instability at greater pH values [24,25,48]. Anthocyanins undergo degradation from quinoidal base (anhydrous), flavylium cations, carbinol base (colorless), and chalcone (yellowish) at pH values between 5 and 7 [48] (Fig. 8). The research findings demonstrate that quinoidal base and chalcone play a significant role in the transformation of red/purple anthocyanins to yellow cation [24,25,48].

Bacterial breakdown and muscle deterioration produced glycosidases, peroxidases, and alkaline compounds during storage [48]. Glycosidases and peroxidases are primarily responsible for the degradation of anthocyanins [31]. It is important to note that glycosidases significantly affect the overall stability of anthocyanin (Fig. 8), resulting in a significant color shift [48,49].

Compared to the neat CAR film, CAR/RP-CD/RPA films last without loss of structural integrity inside stored food boxes, which can be related to the antibacterial and antioxidant properties of RP-CDs incorporated into real-time intelligent indicator films for quality monitoring of stored food models.

Red rose petal anthocyanin (RPA) was extracted using optimized protocols using Box-Behnken modeling, with the higher TAC (159.2 mg/100 g), DPPH (70.1 %), and ABTS (81.5 %), scavenging action at time of 11.15 min, the ethanolic concentration of 68 % and temperature of 73.7 °C. RP-based carbon dots (RP-CDs) with highly fluorescent, UV-absorbent, stable (with −23.1 mV zeta potential), and antimicrobial and antioxidant efficiencies were prepared using a green hydrothermal (180 °C/7 h) method for active packaging applications. Adding RPA (5 %) and RP-CDs (2.0 %) to the carrageenan-based film improved film properties with enhanced thermal stability. The addition of RP-CDs and RPA significantly enhanced the WCA, WVP (by 60 %), UV barrier (>193 %), and antioxidant scavenging of the carrageenan film. Food storage applications (In vivo model) studies discovered that the composite film functioned as an active film and monitored the freshness in real-time by visual intelligible/quantifiable changes from red to yellowish. Anthocyanin-doped carrageenan-based nanocomposites (CDs) and anthocyanin-rich packaging materials have high potential in food packaging applications, especially in new active and intelligent packaging with multifunctional properties, promoting significant development of smart packaging. Future research needs to focus on scaling intelligent packaging from the laboratory to industry, increasing cost-effectiveness, addressing regulatory aspects, and strengthening consumer acceptance to make intelligent packaging commercially viable, effective, and sustainable.
